coredata.dc:title: Surface relaxation determine the band bending and special optical properties of carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Yang
Ji, Zheng
Tian, Fengguo
Peng, Cheng
Wu, Biao
Liu, Xiaoya
Liu, Qizhen
Wu, Jiang
Wang, Xuzhuo
Qi, Xuemei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDots) are frequently adopted to modify composite photocatalysts, due to their unique optical properties and adjustable band structure. Here, an ab initio approach based on extend Hückel theory is used to study in detail the dependent relationship between the optical properties of carbon nanodots and surface relaxation. In order to imitate the surface structure of carbon nanodots, a number of two-layer tiny carbon domains are used. The energy level distribution shows a clear band gap, demonstrating the features of semiconductor. The band structure is somewhat bent as a result of surface relaxation's dramatic effect on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The ultraviolet and visible absorption spectra of carbon nanodots are successfully predicted in theory, by considering the oscillator strength in every possibility transition, and the transition from the valence band maximum (VBM) to conduction band minimum (CBM) is the main peak location. The surface relaxation results in red shift of the spectral lines, which is the potential reason why carbon nanodots promote visible light absorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Photocatalysis
Ab initio
Extend Hückel theory

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of a carbon nanodot; (b) Carbon domains on the surface of carbon nanodots.
Graphical representation of the overlapping integral matrix S and Hamiltonian matrix H of CDots-4.
Energy level diagrams for each carbon domain with surface relaxation. (Blue is the valence band, and red is the conduction band. The dashed line shows the Fermi level position).
HOMO (a), LUMO (b) and HOMO-LUMO gap (c) for each carbon domain with surface relaxation.
(a) UV-vis spectra of each carbon domain with fixed interlayer spacing of 3.4 Å; (b) UV-vis spectra of CDots-4.

head.ce:title: Surface relaxation determine the band bending and special optical properties of carbon nanodots

Corresponding author at: No. 2103 Pingliang Road, Shanghai 200090, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDots) are frequently adopted to modify composite photocatalysts, due to their unique optical properties and adjustable band structure. Here, an ab initio approach based on extend Hückel theory is used to study in detail the dependent relationship between the optical properties of carbon nanodots and surface relaxation. In order to imitate the surface structure of carbon nanodots, a number of two-layer tiny carbon domains are used. The energy level distribution shows a clear band gap, demonstrating the features of semiconductor. The band structure is somewhat bent as a result of surface relaxation's dramatic effect on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The ultraviolet and visible absorption spectra of carbon nanodots are successfully predicted in theory, by considering the oscillator strength in every possibility transition, and the transition from the valence band maximum (VBM) to conduction band minimum (CBM) is the main peak location. The surface relaxation results in red shift of the spectral lines, which is the potential reason why carbon nanodots promote visible light absorption.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDots), due to its unique optical properties, microscopic morphology, non-toxic and eco-friendly nature, and superior electronic transmission characteristics, have received a lot of interest [1–5]. Particularly, carbon nanodots differ from bulk carbon due to their small size and quantum confinement effect with adjustable band structure, which can be used to modify composite photocatalytic systems and are widely applied in the photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide [6–8], photohydrolytic hydrogen production [9–11], photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [12–14], and photocatalytic removal of heavy metal pollutants [15–17], among other processes. A carbon nanodots-silica hybrid photocatalyst with high CO2 reduction activity and selectivity was created by Yadav et al. [6] using a simple, environmentally friendly reverse microemulsion process. The surface modification and graphitization of CDots had a significant impact on their photocatalytic efficacy [7]. Chen et al. [9] introduced carbon dots to modify the Cu-In-Zn-S and MoS2 photocatalyst, and obtained enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production rate, which was attributed to the enhanced charge transfer process due to the electron sinking effect of carbon dots. Zhou et al. [12] prepared aerogel-like carbon dots and applied them to photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants, obtaining a better degradation rate constant than graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4). Carbon nanodots also have applications in heavy metal pollution control. He et al. [15] modified MIL-53(Fe)/noble metal with carbon nanodots to generate ternary nanocomposites, which have been utilized effectively to remove heavy metal Cr(VI) from industrial sewage. Wu et al. [17] cleverly introduced carbon spheres in the hydrothermal preparation process to modify BiOIO3 and form heterojunction, thus obtaining enhanced photocatalytic oxidation performance for elemental mercury removal under visible light.

Experimental researchers usually taken characterization methods, such as ultraviolet and visible absorption spectra (UV-vis) and photoluminescence spectra (PL), to describe the optical properties of carbon nanodots and their composite materials [8,11,13]. One often attributed the mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the composite system to the fact that carbon nanodots facilitate the absorption of visible light, regulate the band structure of the system, and promote the migration of photogenerated carriers [1,18]. Theoretical analysis, in addition to experimental study, may more thoroughly explain the internal physical mechanism and better direct the manufacture of carbon nanodots and the design of the experimental setup [19–22].

Since carbon nanodots have quantum effect due to their small size, the ab initio computation must be introduced to effectively characterize their structure-activity relationship. Considering that carbon nanodots are composed of carbon atoms which form complex orbital overlaps and chemical bonds with each other, using the extended Hückel theory (EHT) is an effective calculation method [23,24], which strikes a balance between accuracy and computational power. Lee et al. [25] combined the Extended Hückel method and Slater's rules for the initial guess of electron density, and implemented all-electron real-space finite element Kohn-Sham density functional theory calculation which obtained faster self consistent field convergence rate. Ribeiro et al. [26] has successfully calculated the electronic structure of III-V semiconductors compounds and their alloys based on extended Hückel theory, while the theoretical results were well combined with the experimental values. Zienert et al. [27] calculated the band structures and electron transport properties of carbon nanotubes with different sizes, and the obtained parameters had good portability and transferable. Tepliakov et al. [28] skillfully simulated the small surface domains of carbon nanodots with four benzene rings in a double layer, and studied the impact of various degrees of hybridization between sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms on the optical properties of the system. Nevertheless, the carbon nanodots face special physical and chemical environment where the surface carbon atoms on one side are exposed to the atmosphere, and the other side is attracted by the carbon atoms inside the bulk. The imbalance of internal and external forces will lead to the tendency of surface carbon atoms to move away from bulk carbon, resulting in elongated layer spacing to reduce the total energy of the system, that so-called the surface relaxation phenomenon [29,30]. However, there are few reports of how surface relaxation affects the optical properties of carbon nanodots.

In this work, the dependent relationship between surface relaxation and optical properties of carbon nanodots was studied in detail via ab initio calculation based on extended Hückel theory. Small carbon domains composed of benzene rings with different number and spatial structure were used to simulate the surface structure of carbon nanodots, and the total wave function of the system was expanded by STO-3G basis set. According to the linear variational principle of quantum mechanics, the energy level distribution of carbon nanodots was obtained, and the expansion coefficient of the corresponding wave function were clarified. The variation of band gap with surface relaxation of carbon nanodots was analyzed. The UV-vis absorption spectra of carbon nanodots were predicted theoretically via the superposition of oscillator strength at every possible energy level transition. This study offers a theoretical foundation for understanding the exceptional optical properties of carbon nanodots, and gives some guidance for the effective synthesis of carbon nanodots composite photocatalysts.

Carbon nanodot structures have quantum effects, which need to be analyzed from the ab initio perspective. Once the time-independent Schrödinger equation for the carbon nanodot structure is solved and the analytic value of the wave function is obtained, then the properties of the system can be confirmed and analyzed. In consideration of the complexity of solving Schrödinger equation directly, this work adopts the extended Hückel theory (EHT) [23,24].

Firstly, an appropriate basis set wave function is selected, and the total wave function of the system can be expanded into a linear combination of the basis set (Eq. 1). The expansion coefficient ci is undetermined, and the resulting total wave function is the trial wave function. In particular, the method of expanding molecular orbitals by linearly combined of atomic orbital wave functions is so-named the LCAO-MO method [31,32].
(1)
ψ
(
r →
)
=
∑
i = 1
N
c i
φ
(
r →
)

The mathematical expectation of the system Hamiltonian is obtained by Eq. 2, namely, the energy value of the system. Considering that the trial total wave function is not orthogonal normalization, a normalization factor is added to the denominator. By substituting the trial total wave function with the linear combination of bases, and the expansion is obtained accordingly.
(2)
E =

where, Sij in the denominator is the overlapping integral, which value is equal to the integral in the full real space of the wave function product of the atomic orbital i and j (Eq. (3)).

The calculation of Hij is relatively complex that according to the extended Hückel theory, the specific value of Hij can be discussed in two cases: When i = j, it should be equal to the first ionization energy of the specific element (Table S1, Supplementary Material) [23,27,28]; When i ≠ j, it can be calculated according to Eq. (4).
(4)
H
i j
= K
H
i i
+
H
j j
2
S
i j
where, K is the correction coefficient, usually ranging 1.0 ∼ 1.75. In this paper, K = 1.0 is used, which obtain better calculation results [23,28]. According to the principle of linear variational principle of quantum mechanics [33,34], one need to solve the secular equation in Eq. 5.
(5)
∑
j = 1
N
(
H
i j
−
E i
S
i j
)
c j
= 0
(
i = 1 , 2 , 3 , ⋯ , n
)

The secular equation can also be written in matrix multiplication form like HC = ESC, where E is the eigenvalue, which represents the allowable energy value of the system. C is the coefficient matrix, which is filled with the coefficients of each atomic orbital wave function that determines the total wave function. By observing the calculation formula of Hij (Eq. 4), it can be found that Sij must be calculated first, and then Hij can be obtained after substitution. The equation HC = ESC, is equivalent to solving the generalized eigenvalue and generalized eigenvector problem, where E is the generalized eigenvalue of H relative to S, and C is the generalized eigenvector belonging to E. The generalized eigenvalue E should satisfy that the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero, that obey
det (
H − E S
) = 0
. Thus, a series of desirable eigenvalues E 1,E 2,⋅⋅⋅, EN can be obtained, which are the possible values of the system energy. For each energy levelEk , one can inversely find the orbital coefficients Ck = [c 1,c 2,⋅⋅⋅, cN ] T in LCAO-MO method for the specific energy level k. Substituting the coefficients of atomic orbitals into Eq. 1, the total wave function of a specific energy level can be obtained.

In this paper, the Slater type orbital (STO) basis set is used to describe the atomic orbitals [35–37]. Besides, in order to avoid the difficulty of calculating the triple integral of polycenter of STO, it is feasible to use Gaussian type orbital (GTO) to fit the STO [38,39]. Thus, this work adopts three GTO functions to fit one STO function, namely the STO-3G basis set. Please refer to Table S2 for specific fitting coefficients in the Supplementary Material.

With the analytic expression of the wave function, theoretically, the physical quantities of the system can be determined. One of the most important optical properties of the system is the absorption of incident light. It is known that the transition from an energy level α to an energy level β can be characterized by oscillator strength (Eq. (6)) [28,40,41].
(6)
f
α → β
=
2 3
m e
ℏ

E α
)

ξ = x , y , z

)
〉
|
2
where, me is the mass of the electron, and ℏ is the reduced Planck constant, which is equal to h/2π. Then, the absorption of light by the system can be generated by superposition after broadening the intensity of each oscillator, as shown in Eq. (7).
(7)
A
( λ )
∝
∑
α , β
f
α → β
Γ
α → β
( λ )
where, Γα → β(λ) is transition lineshape. Here, the Gaussian type lineshape (or normal distribution) is adopted (Eq. (8)).
(8)
Γ
α → β
( λ )
=
1
2 π

e
−
(
λ −
λ
α → β
)
2

σ
2
where, σ is the standard deviation, which value takes 1.0 in this work. λα → β is the wavelength when transition occurs, which is approximately equal to
1240
E β
−
E α
with the energy unit in eV and the wavelength unit in nm.

The carbon nanodots (CDots) model shown in Fig. 1 are adopted, which vary in size and shape. The crystalline phase of carbon also differs; specifically, the surface of the carbon nanodots is more complicated and amorphous while the inside is crystal with regular lattice stripes. Considering that the light used for photocatalysis is distributed in the ultraviolet and visible regions, and the interaction between light and the surface layers of carbon nanodots is more intense, the surface carbon domains model shown in Fig. 1b are abstracted, wherein similar structures have been widely used [18,19,28]. Tepliakov et al. [28], using a two-layer carbon domain consisting of four benzene rings, explained the infrared shift of the spectral absorption peaks of UV-vis and photoluminescence spectra by the sp2-sp3 hybridization of amorphous carbon. Here, we consider several benzene rings and form similar two-layer small carbon domains to characterize the amorphous surface carbon structure of carbon nanodots. Saturate the surrounding carbon with hydrogen atoms to neutralize the unsaturated charge and orbital. The small carbon domains considered are denoted as CDots-x (x=3 ∼ 7), where x is the number of benzene rings. When there are more benzene rings, the small domains often have different configurations, that distinguished by the Roman numerals I, II and III. For easy observation, carbon atoms on the outer and sub-outer surfaces are marked in red and blue color, respectively. The hydrogen atoms around it are colored in white. All atoms in each system are numbered in order to write and H and S matrices, as shown in Table S3. Given that the chemical environments that the carbon on the surface and the carbon inside the carbon nanodots are exposed to are very different. In other words, due to the extrusion of adjacent carbons from all sides, the inner carbon exhibits a stable crystal structure under a certain pressure. In transmission electron microscopy, the inner carbon also presents regular geometric stripes, and the layer spacing of the inner carbon is relatively small. The carbon on the surface, one side is exposed to the vacuum, the other side is attracted to the carbon on the inside, so the interlayer spacing is stretched compared to the inner carbon. At the same time, because of the microsize of carbon nanodots, there are many small, irregular carbon structures on their surfaces. We assign each carbon domain a degree of freedom in the direction of interlayer spacing, in the range 1.4 ∼ 5.0 Å, where 1.4 Å is approximately equal to the carbon-carbon bonds length in hydrocarbons and 3.4 Å is the interlayer spacing of graphite crystal. The unique surface structure and surface relaxation benefit of carbon nanodots, as well as their unique optical properties, will be the focus on this study.

The various small carbon domains shown in Fig. 1 have been geometrically optimized based on DFT calculation in advance. After that, the coordinates of each atom in real space are taken as the initial input variables, and the extended Hückel theory calculation is carried out. Firstly, the overlap integral matrix S is calculated according to Eq. 3, and then the Hamiltonian matrix H is calculated through Eq. 4. Taking CDots-4 structure as an example, S and H matrix results are given when the layer spacing is 1.4, 3.4 and 5.0 Å respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 . The numerical values in each element of the matrix are in accordance with the legend, and the order of the elements in the matrix is arranged according to the numbering rules in Table S3. By observing the graphic representation of the overlapping integral matrix S, it can be seen that the elements in the diagonal position of the matrix are all one no matter what the layer spacing is, which is because the atomic orbitals adopted here are all orthogonal normalized. Most of the values in the matrices S and H are zero, and only some blocks have values. This is also easy to understand, combined with the structure diagram of the small domains of carbon nanodots, only when the two atoms are close to each other, their wave function orbits will form strong overlap, which is consistent with physical intuition. In addition, it is found that with the increasing of layer spacing (1.4 → 5.0 Å), the color of some blocks in the matrix gradually fades and eventually becomes zero. This can be related to the interaction between the two carbon layers: As the distance between layers gradually increases, the interaction between layers gradually decreases, which slows down the overlap of electron clouds, resulting in changes in the electronic structure of the system. There are also blocks in the matrix whose values change relatively little and remain stable, due to the interactions between the carbon skeletons in each layer. Because the contribution of each valence electron to the energy level of the system is considered by using the extended Hückel method, the characterization of the electronic structure is more rich and delicate, compared with the traditional Hückel method. Moreover, the traditional Hückel method is only suitable for the monolayer conjugated bond system, not suitable for the surface relaxation model of this work.

Thus, the secular equation Eq. 5 is transformed into the solution of equations (H − ES)C = 0, i.e., the generalized eigenvalues and eigenvectors of HC = ESC. The eigenvalues obtained are the possible energy values that the system might have, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Energy is discontinuous distribution and can only take separate states, that is, energy is quantized, which is an inevitable requirement of the principle of quantum mechanics, clearly different from the classical theory. However, it can be discovered that the energy level of CDots-3 is comparatively loose, and a clearly discrete energy level can be seen. With the increase of the number of benzene rings in the small carbon domains, the arrangement of energy levels becomes more dense. In the CDots-7 structure, energy levels are so dense that it is difficult to observe discrete energy levels, thus presenting a quasi-continuous band distribution in a specific energy interval, so-called the band structure. This is because when the size of the carbon domain increases, the number of atoms in the system increases accordingly, the number of valence electron orbitals increases greatly and the scale of overlapping integral matrix and Hamiltonian matrix also becomes larger. The more complex atomic environment also leads to a decrease in the degeneracy of the system energy levels. The degeneracy levels are destroyed in large numbers, increasing the level dispersion and making it appear as if energy is continuously distributed.

The occupied molecular orbital (OMO) can be divided mainly into two valence bands. Among them, the energy level distribution of one valence band is wide in the energy range of -26 ∼ -22 eV, which is explained by the stable overlap of electron clouds creating the carbon skeleton. Since they are less energetic and consequently inactive, these electrons are not actively involved in chemical reactions. There is also a relatively thin valence band around -13 eV, which is the valence electron of the system with relatively high energy and high chemical reactivity. On the other hand, the unoccupied molecular orbital (UMO), are generally distributed over a broad energy range of -11 ∼ -7 eV. Surface relaxation can have some impact on the distribution of energy levels. When the interlayer spacing is small (1.4 Å), the energy level distribution is relatively large. While, with the increasing of interlayer spacing (1.4 → 5.0 Å), the energy levels become more compressed and compact. When the interlayer spacing is small, the change of energy level is obvious. With the continuous increase of interlayer spacing, the energy level changes are not significant. This is due to the fact that the two carbon structures are far apart and interact with each other less and less, and the limit is to form two separate graphene sheets.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) are investigated under the influence of surface relaxation, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . It is evident that the HOMO level position is greatly affected by surface relaxation (Fig. 4a). With the continuous increase of interlayer spacing (1.4 → 5.0 Å), the HOMO level first increases and then decreases. The HOMO level reaches its maximum when the interlayer spacing are 2.2 Å. The HOMO changes significantly when the interlayer spacing is in the range of 1.4 ∼ 3.4 Å. As the distance widened further (3.4 → 5.0 Å), the HOMO slowly decreased until it leveled off. The energy level distribution is also related to the number of benzene rings in the carbon domains. Even if the number of benzene rings is consistent in a small domain, the structure of isomers will also affect the distribution of energy levels. In comparison, the distribution of the LUMO is relatively concentrated, with a value of about -11.4 eV (Fig. 4b). The HOMO distribution of carbon nanodots is greatly affected by surface relaxation, while the LUMO remains relatively stable. Fig. 4c shows the distribution of HOMO-LUMO gap, indicating that the energy band gap of carbon nanodots is not always constant. The surface relaxation phenomenon can cause the band bending of carbon nanodots. When the interlayer spacing changes from 1.4 to 2.2 Å, the energy band gap decreases gradually. With the further widening of the layer distance, the band gap rises slowly, which is consistent with the above analysis. Surface relaxation phenomenon can bend the energy band gap of carbon nanodots, which provide inspiration for modifying the optical response and properties of the compound system.

The presence of carbon nanodots can alter the response characteristics of the composite system to radiation light, affect the optical properties and thus change the function of structure. According to Eq. 6, the oscillator strength of each carbon domain is calculated, and then the Gaussian type linear expansion is carried out to obtain the UV-vis absorption spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S1 (in the Supplementary Material).

Fig. 5a is the UV-vis spectra of each carbon domain, with a fixed interlayer spacing of 3.4 Å. It can be seen that the absorption of light by carbon nanodots is mainly in the range of ultraviolet and visible light (< 800 nm). As the wavelength continues to increase (> 1000 nm), the light absorption of the system gradually decreases and eventually approaches zero. This makes sense given that carbon nanodots have small structures, localized quantum advantages, and a band gap width that can be regarded as local semiconductors. The existence of the band gap width results in that only radiation with a specific energy may excite carbon nanodots, and cause them to absorb radiation. As a result, far infrared light with lower energy cannot activate the carbon nanodots. This similar phenomenon is also consistent with experimental observations [7,13,16,18,42-45]. In the UV-visible region, two main absorption peaks can be observed. In the ultraviolet region, the absorption peak of about 265 nm can be attributed to the transition from valence band (∼ -13 eV) to higher conduction band (∼ -8 eV). There is also a significant absorption peak at about 570 nm in the visible region, which corresponds to the transition from the valence band maximum (VBM, ∼ -13 eV) to the conduction band minimum (CBM, ∼ 11 eV). There is also a transition from a low-energy occupied orbital (around -26 ∼ -22eV) to an unoccupied orbital (-11 ∼ -7 eV). Such transitions require very high radiation energy (> 11 eV) and cannot be excited in the ultraviolet and visible range, so they are not shown in the spectra. In addition, it can also be observed that the absorption of radiated light by the structure gradually increases with the increasing number of benzene rings in the carbon domains. The specific structure of carbon domains also affects the details of UV-vis spectra, but the general trend is consistent. Fig. 5b shows the UV-vis absorption spectra of the CDots-4 structure under the action of surface relaxation. The distribution of light absorption by CDots-4 is still mostly in the UV-visible spectrum. With the increase of surface layer spacing in carbon domain, it can be found that the response to visible light is slightly enhanced, i.e., the spectral line red shift phenomenon arises. This offers a fresh explanation for why carbon nanodot-rich composite photocatalytic systems work better than similar systems without them: the intricately shaped, microscopic carbon nanodots have special quantum properties that promote visible light absorption.

In summary, the optical properties and structure-activity relationships of carbon nanodots have been carefully studied from an ab initio perspective. The electronic structure of carbon nanodots can be well described by the extend Hückel theory, which is based on the linear variational principle of quantum mechanics. When the size of carbon domain increases, the energy level distribution gradually shifts from discrete to quasi-continuous distribution, presenting the so-called band structure. The HOMO of carbon nanodots varies greatly with surface relaxation, while The change in LUMO is not significant. The distribution of UV-vis spectra is the comprehensive result of the oscillator strength in each transition, and the transition from the VBM to CBM is the location of the main peak. The surface relaxation results in red shift of the spectral lines, and the absorption of visible light is enhanced by carbon nanodots. Surface relaxation can bend the energy band gap of carbon nanodots, which sheds light on the optical response and properties of composite systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52076126), National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFE0109800), and Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (22010501500), Key Laboratory of Clean Power Generation and Environmental Protection Technology in Mechanical Industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at 10.1016/j.surfin.2022.102338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots, Unconventional Preparation Strategies, and Applications Beyond Photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xue-Tao Tian, Xue-Bo Yin. Carbon Dots, Unconventional Preparation Strategies, and Applications Beyond Photoluminescence. Small, 2019, 15(48): 1901803.
